coredata.dc:title: Using carbon quantum dots to improve the resistive switching behavior of ZnO nanorods device

Xu, Jianping
Shi, Shaobo
Zhang, Xiaosong
Zhang, Xuguang
Shi, Xin
Li, Shubin
Li, Linlin
Liu, Xiaojuan
Li, Lan
coredata.dc:description:
An electronic bistable device with a composite structure was fabricated using tapered ZnO nanorod arrays (ZnO NRs) coated with carbon quantum-dots (C QDs). With the addition of C QDs, the ON/OFF resistance ratio is 6 ×
10
2
, over 100 times higher than that of device with pristine ZnO NRs. The effect of C QDs on the resistive switching behavior was investigated by current–voltage (I–V) and capacitance–voltage (C–V) characterization. The conduction mechanisms of the devices were discussed using space charge limited current (SCLC) model.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Composite structure
ZnO nanorod
Carbon quantum-dots
ON/OFF resistance ratio
Conduction mechanisms

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (Color online.) Schematic diagram for the preparation procedure of ZnO NRs/C QDs composite layer and the device structure.

(Color online.) (a) The XRD patterns of the as-prepared ZnO NRs/C QDs composite film. The diffraction peak of 2θ =21.4° is related to the bottom electrode of ITO film. (b) The FTIR spectrum for the as-prepared ZnO NRs/C QDs composite film.

(Color online.) (a) The cross-sectional view and top-view (the inset) image of as-prepared ZnO nanorod. (b) The AFM image of the C QDs. (c) The top-view image and EDS data (the inset) of the composite film. (d) The cross-sectional image and line-scan EDS (the inset) of the hybrid film.

(Color online.) (a) The I–V curves for device ITO/ZnO NRs/Al, ITO/C QDs/Al and ITO/ZnO NRs (C QDs)/Al, and the inset shows the device yield of device ITO/ZnO NRs/Al and ITO/ZnO NRs (C QDs)/Al. (b) The J–V curves of HRS at positive voltage region in double logarithmic scale. (c) The normalized high frequency C–V curves under ±5 V sweep voltage for the two devices. (d) The photoluminescence spectra of ZnO NRs, C QDs and ZnO NRs/C QDs.
(Color online.) Schematic diagrams of the electron transport processes in the ITO/ZnO NRs/Al and ITO/ZnO NRs (C QDs)/Al devices.
(Color online.) The retention properties of device ITO/ZnO NRs/Al and ITO/ZnO NRs (C QDs)/Al at the read voltage of 0.1 V.

head.ce:title: Using carbon quantum dots to improve the resistive switching behavior of ZnO nanorods device

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Institute of Material Physics, Key Laboratory of Display Materials and Photoelectric Devices, Ministry of Education, Tianjin University of Technology, Tianjin 300384, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Display Materials and Photoelectric Devices

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Science, Tianjin University of Technology and Education, Tianjin 300222, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Tianjin University of Technology and Education

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para:
An electronic bistable device with a composite structure was fabricated using tapered ZnO nanorod arrays (ZnO NRs) coated with carbon quantum-dots (C QDs). With the addition of C QDs, the ON/OFF resistance ratio is 6 ×
10
2
, over 100 times higher than that of device with pristine ZnO NRs. The effect of C QDs on the resistive switching behavior was investigated by current–voltage (I–V) and capacitance–voltage (C–V) characterization. The conduction mechanisms of the devices were discussed using space charge limited current (SCLC) model.

ce:section.ce:para: The electronic bistable device is expected to have high storage density, fast switching speed and low power consumption [1–3]. In recent years, the organic materials [4] and nanostructure [5] have attracted great interests as potential resistive switching materials for integrated and flexible electronics applications. Among them, one-dimensional (1D) nanostructure has been extensively explored to realize the narrow dispersion in ON and OFF resistance states and a high switching endurance [6] induced by space-confined carriers transport. In 2010, Kazuki Nagashima et al. reported a small dispersion in the resistance value of ON and OFF states and the switching endurance as high as 108 times was obtained in the memory device with cobalt oxide nanowires [6]. 1D ZnO prepared by soft chemical method has abundant surface defects and has been recently considered a good candidate for the electronic bistable devices [7]. However the small ON/OFF ratio and low device yield limit their application in ZnO based electronic bistable devices [8]. Two methods such as designing a new device structure [9] and optimizing the device fabrication process have been proposed to improve the performance of the devices [10]. Chuang et al. reported the ON/OFF ratio of the device is up to 105 using a bridge structure between ZnO NRs. By coating CeO2 quantum dots on the surface of ZnO NRs, Younis et al. improved ON/OFF ratio to 102 [10]. As is well known, the gaps among the ZnO nanorods may induce the possible contact between the top and bottom electrodes and then result in the low yield in ZnO nanorod based electronic bistable devices. Therefore, dense nanorods growth or gap filling strategy are the effectively methods to prevent the leak current and improve the performance of the devices [8,11].

Recently, carbon quantum-dots (C QDs) have become a promising material for photocatalysis, solar cells, biomaterials etc. because of its low-toxicity and abundant physical and chemical properties [12,13]. Besides, C QDs can help to trap and transfer the electrons by modifying various organic and inorganic materials [14], which is important in enhancing the activity of the semiconductor optoelectronic and electronic devices. In this paper, C QDs were introduced into the ZnO NRs to improve the electrical bistable device characteristics. The switching mechanism was elucidated by I–V and C–V measurements and the influence of C QDs on the device performance was discussed.

The indium tin oxide (ITO) electrode was cleaned sequentially by using liquid detergent, deionized water, acetone, isopropanol and ethanol under ultrasonic agitation. After that, the ZnO NRs seed solution, which was prepared by the method of Claudia Pacholski et al. [15], was spin coated on ITO glass at 2500 rpm for 30 s, followed by the baking temperature at 80 °C for 15 min. After repeated for three times, the as-prepared seed layer film was fixed upside down in 400 ml mixture solution consisting of 0.15 M zinc nitrate hexahydrate (Zn(NO3)2⋅6H2O) and 0.1 M hexamethylenetetramine (HMT, C6H12N4). ZnO NRs firstly grew at 75 °C for 1.5 h and then kept at 95 °C for 1.5 h. The as-grown sample was rinsed thoroughly with deionized water to remove the remaining residue and then dried in vacuum at 80 °C.

We prepared C QDs by the method of Fu Wang et al. [16]. 1 ml C QDs toluene solution with a concentration of 0.1 mg/ml was dropped on the surface of ZnO NRs film, and subsequent dried in vacuum at 80 °C for 2 h. Then 0.5 ml toluene C QDs solution was spin-coated on the film at the speed of 500 rpm for 15 s and also dried in vacuum at 80 °C for 30 min. Finally, an Al top electrode with thickness about 150 nm was deposited on as-prepared film by thermal evaporation under high vacuum of 5 ×
10
− 4
Pa . The active area was defined with a shadow mask and measured 2 × 2
mm
2
. The schematic diagram of the device structure is depicted in Fig. 1 in which the hybrid ZnO NRs/C QDs film is sandwiched by two electrodes. The similar fabrication procedure was employed for the original device without C QDs.

The crystal structure of the composite film was characterized by using Rigaku 2500/PC x-ray diffractometer (XRD) with Cu Ka radiation source (0.1542 nm) at 40 kV and 150 mA. The molecular structure and possible chemical bonding of the composite film were investigated by Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectrometer. The morphology of the ZnO nanorods and the composite film was conducted by using field emission scanning electron microscopy (FESEM, JEOL JSM-71001F). The size of C QDs was estimated by atomic force microscopy (BRUKER INNOVA). The elemental composition was analyzed by energy dispersive spectrometer (EDS). Jobin Yvon FluoroLog-3 fluorescence spectrometer was employed for the characterization of the electrons transition in films. The resistive switching properties of the device were performed with semiconductor performance analyzer (Keithley 4200). In the process of the voltage sweep, the bias was defined as positive when the current flowed from the bottom electrode to the top electrode. All the measurements were carried out at room temperature.

The XRD patterns of the as-prepared ZnO NRs/C QDs composite film are shown in Fig. 2 (a) in which the sharp diffraction peaks match with the typical wurtzite ZnO phase (JCPDS 36-1451). In accordance with Hang et al. [14], the broad envelope at ∼26° can be related to C QDs. The FTIR spectrum can characterize the surface bonding of the composite film, as shown in Fig. 2(b). The band at around 532 cm−1 is reasonably associated with the stretching vibration of ZnO bond [14]. The peak at 1703 cm−1 is related to CO vibration. The low energy absorption bands corresponding to 3075–2732 cm−1 and 3400–3130 cm−1 are representing the stretching vibration of CH and NH, respectively. The morphology of as-grown ZnO NRs sample is shown in Fig. 3 (a) with the inset planar surface. The sample is 1.3 μm length and the cone-shaped appearance may provide enough space for adventive nanoparticles. From AFM image in Fig. 3(b), we can estimate that the diameter of the C QDs is around 8 nm. Fig. 3(c) gives the top view of the composite film. In comparison with as-grown ZnO NRs in inset of Fig. 3(a), the film surface becomes compact and smooth. The exposed tips of ZnO NRs may be especially suitable for the electrical contact to the Al electrode. The EDS data (inset of Fig. 3(c)) shows evidence of high zinc and low oxygen concentrations which are characteristic of oxygen vacancies or zinc interstitial defects in ZnO by chemical preparation [7]. The sectional view of the composite film, as shown in Fig. 3(d), revealed the filled gaps between nanorods by extra C QDs. The line-scan EDS profiles along the nanorod in the inset clearly show a dramatic decrease in the Zn and corresponding increase in C content when covered by C QDs.

The I–V curves for ITO/ZnO NRs/Al, ITO/C QDs/Al and ITO/ZnO NRs (C QDs)/Al devices were shown in Fig. 4 (a). The devices except for ITO/C QDs/Al display a stable resistive switching phenomenon with the voltage scanning 0 V → 5 V → 0 V → −5 V → 0 V. In the ITO/ZnO NRs (C QDs)/Al device, the current value shows the steady increase after the voltage applying on the device. An abrupt enhancement of current was observed at about 1.9 V (set voltage), representing the switching from the high resistance state (HRS) to the low resistance state (LRS). The device remains in the LRS during voltage sweep 1.9 V → 5 V → −2.7 V and then gives negative differential resistance effect by a sudden drop in current at −2.7 V (reset voltage), indicating the switching from LRS back to HRS. The maximum resistance ratio between the LRS and the HRS is about 6 ×
10
2
, over 100 times higher than that of reference device with single ZnO NRs. As described by Wang et al. [16], with the numerous groups such as CO and CH at the surface, C QDs have abundant trapping defects for electrons. The introduction of C QDs on the surface of ZnO NRs can enhance charge trapping of ZnO NRs. The yields for two devices are statistically displayed in the inset of Fig. 4(a). With the addition of C QDs, the device yield was improved from 15% to about 90% and this may be due to the filling C QDs between the interspaces of ZnO NRs.

The J–V curves at positive voltage region in double logarithmic scale are given in Fig. 4(b) to clarify the conduction mechanism after C QDs addition. The C QDs contained device shows significantly lower current density than the reference device under the same voltage range. Ohmic conduction characteristic with the slope of ∼1 is dominated at the low voltage range in the HRS for both devices. The curve slopes increase to ∼2 with elevated voltage, suggesting the conduction mechanism change to space charge limited current (SCLC) [11]. In addition, a third region with a slope value of ∼24 was observed for device ITO/ZnO NRs(C QDs)/Al when positive voltage is applied larger than 2.3 V. The phenomenon reveals the fact that the trapped charge limited current (TCLC) is related to the extra traps provided by C QDs.

The SCLC mechanism is generally attributed to the presence of trapping centers and can be described by the equation [17]:
(1)
J =
(
θ
θ + 1
)
9 μ
ε
γ
ε
0
V
2
8
L
3
where θ represents the ratio of the free electrons and the trapped electrons. The relevant coefficients are denoted as Boltzmann constant K, vacuum dielectric constant
ε
0
, static permittivity of the material of
ε
γ
and the electron mobility of μ. When most of the injected electrons are eventually trapped by numbers of traps, θ ≪ 1 and the equation (1) is represented by the typical equation J = θ
9 μ
ε
γ
ε
0
V
2
8
L
3
. At the similar total thickness and applied voltage, lower θ is obtained in ITO/ZnO NRs(C QDs)/Al device because of the lower current density at HRS, suggesting that there are more trapping electrons in the device with C QDs.

The series capacitor is commonly dependent on the charge-storage ability, representing the trapping and detrapping of electrons in the device. The normalized high frequency (1 MHz) C–V curves under ±5 V sweep voltage for the two devices were measured and depicted in Fig. 4(c). The counterclockwise hysteresis behavior was observed in both devices while the addition of C QDs induces a larger hysteresis window. The trapped charge density N can be estimated by the equation [18]:
(2)
N =
Δ
V
FB
C
ox
q A
where
C
ox
is oxide capacitance, A is electrode area and q is the charge of electron or hole. The charge trapping density in device without C QDs coating was calculated to be 5.63 ×
10
11
cm
− 2
from flat-band voltage shift ( Δ
V
FB
) of 2.7 V, which is about one-fold higher than the value ( 4.09 ×
10
10
cm
− 2
) in the reference device with Δ
V
FB
(0.2 V). This is particularly relevant with the more trapping defects in the device with C QDs coating.

The photoluminescence spectra (PL) of ZnO NRs, C QDs and ZnO NRs/C QDs hybrid films are shown in Fig. 4(d). ZnO NRs exhibit a weak near-band edge emission (NBE) [7] at 378 nm and a strong visible emission at 560 nm. The visible emission is commonly attributed to surface states and defects. For C QDs, the broad visible emission at ∼445 nm can be observed, which can be assigned to surface states [13]. C QDs coating causes a pronounced decrease of defects related visible emission [19] and increases the trapped electrons in the hybrid films, which improves the device properties.

The electron transport processes in the two devices are comparatively described and discussed according to Fig. 5 . The energy level offsets between ZnO with Al and ITO are 0.1 eV and 2.6 eV, respectively. The energy level discrepancy may prevent holes diffusion under applied electric field. The numerous defects existing on the surface of n-type ZnO NRs can induce the carriers (especially electrons in this device structure) trapping and de-trapping effect which results in the switching between ON and OFF states [4]. The switching phenomena between ON and OFF state for the reference device is illustrated in Fig. 5(a) to 5(c). The injected electrons from Al electrode can be captured and partly occupy the traps under positive bias to behave as the high resistance state. With the voltage increase, the traps are fully filled with electrons. The electrons can directly transfer to ITO electrode and a low resistance state is obtained. Under negative bias, the captured electrons are released back to the Al electrode, resulting in the switching to high resistance state. A similar process is described in Fig. 5(d)–5(f) for the device with C QDs addition. The C QDs with numerous groups such as CO and CH at the surface can introduce more trapping defects on the surface of ZnO NRs and then enhance charge trapping density. The total defect traps within the forbidden gap in device were redistributed exponentially by these introduced traps, leading to the TCLC mechanism [20]. The set voltage, which is regarded as the completely filled levels by the injected electrons, is 1.9 V for the device with C QDs coating and 0.4 V for the device with pristine ZnO NRs. The higher set voltage indicates more traps introduced by C QDs.

The retention properties were further comparatively measured for ITO/ZnO NRs/Al and ITO/ZnO NRs (C QDs)/Al device. Fig. 6 revealed that, the two devices show similar current stability in the time range of 104 s for ON and OFF states. Compared to the reference device, the current value at OFF state for the device with C QDs coating is much smaller, which can be attributed to the increased trap states induced by C QDs.

In conclusion, an electronic bistable device based on hybrid ZnO NRs/C QDs was demonstrated. For the device with the addition of C QDs, the ON/OFF resistance ratio was over 100 times larger than that of the device without C QDs coating. But there is no obvious difference in current stability at the time range of 104 s for ON and OFF states. The resistive switching behavior was explained on the basis of the space-charge-limited current (SCLC) mechanism. The charge trapping density in device without C QDs coating is about one-fold higher than that of the reference device, indicating more trapping defects in the device with C QDs addition.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National High Technology Research and Development Program of China (863 Program) (Grant No. 2013AA014201), the Natural Science Foundation of Tianjin (Grant Nos. 11JCYBJC00300, 14JCZDJC31200, 15JCYBJC16700 and 15JCYBJC16800), the National Key Foundation for Exploring Scientific Instrument of China (Grant No. 2014YQ120351) and International Cooperation Program from Science and Technology of Tianjin (Grant No. 14RCGHGX00872).
